title: The Heavenly Pivot
content:
The Jade Emperor sat at the head of the conference table, his celestial robes swapped for a navy blue blazer with brass buttons. The room was cold. Not the cold of the Ninth Heaven, but the cold of an overworked air conditioner that hadn't been serviced since the Tang Dynasty.

"We're burning through incense faster than we're collecting it," he said, sliding a tablet across the table. The screen showed a graph that looked like a heart attack. "We need a pivot."

Guan Yu's face, already the color of aged cherrywood, darkened further. His beard bristled. "I'm not doing TikTok."

"No one said TikTok," the Jade Emperor said. "Though if we're being honest, your saber demonstrations could—"

"No."

The Heavenly Court's main hall had been converted into something resembling a WeWork space. Whiteboards covered the walls where cloud patterns used to drift. Someone had installed a ping pong table where the Peach Garden used to be. The Eight Immortals sat in ergonomic chairs that squeaked every time someone shifted their weight.

Caishen, the God of Wealth, tapped his gold-plated Apple Watch. He'd taken to the mortal world like a duck to a pond full of cash. "The numbers are clear. Our burn rate is unsustainable. We have three months of incense reserves, maybe four if we cut the offerings to minor deities."

"What about the incense from the new temple in Shenzhen?" someone asked.

"What temple?" Caishen's laugh was hollow. "They turned it into a KFC. The Colonel has replaced half our shrines. We're losing market share to fried chicken."

The Jade Emperor pinched the bridge of his nose. He'd aged three thousand years in the last decade. "We need revenue. Real revenue. Mortal currency."

The room went silent.

Nuwa, who had been quietly braiding a strand of her hair into a complex pattern, looked up. Her eyes held the weight of creation. "I've already found work. At a fertility clinic."

Everyone turned.

"It pays well," she said. "And honestly, the work hasn't changed much. Just different materials."

The Kitchen God, Zaojun, cleared his throat. He'd lost weight since the Heavenly Court's budget cuts. "I've been running a food truck outside the tech parks in Hangzhou. The hours are terrible, but the margins are decent."

"Food truck," the Jade Emperor repeated, writing it on a whiteboard. "What else?"

Erlang Shen raised his hand. His third eye was closed, probably to conserve energy. "I've been doing private security. Corporate clients mostly. They hire me to guard data centers."

"And your dog?"

"Xiao Tianquan works the night shift at a warehouse in Pudong. Union job. Benefits."

The Jade Emperor wrote faster. "Anyone else? Be honest. We're past pride."

A low murmur spread through the room. The Eight Immortals exchanged glances. One of them, He Xiangu, spoke up.

"I deliver for Meituan."

"How's that?" the Jade Emperor asked.

"The algorithm is a tyrant. Worse than any demon I've faced. It knows when you slow down. It punishes you for taking bathroom breaks."

From the corner of the room, a voice cut through like a knife through silk.

"The algorithm is nothing."

Everyone turned.

Sun Wukong sat slumped in a chair, his golden fillet replaced with a delivery driver's helmet. His fur was matted. His eyes were tired. The Monkey King, the Great Sage Equal to Heaven, looked like he hadn't slept in a week.

"I've been doing food delivery for two years," he said. "Two years. Do you know what that does to a monkey?"

"You have a 2.1 rating," the Jade Emperor said flatly.

"Because I'm fast. When you're fast, people get suspicious. They think you're going to steal their food. They think you're going to eat it. One time, I delivered a dumpling order in forty seconds flat. The customer complained that I must have dropped it. Who drops a dumpling in forty seconds? You can't even drop a dumpling in forty seconds."

"Wukong, focus."

"I AM FOCUSED." He stood, and for a moment, the old fire flickered in his eyes. "I've been studying them. The demons. They're not stupid. They've adapted."

The room went still.

"What do you mean?" the Jade Emperor asked.

Wukong pulled out his phone. It was cracked in three places. "Look at this. Ride-hailing apps. Food delivery. Freelance platforms. All of it."

He threw the phone on the table. The screen showed an app called "SoulCycle" — a ride-hailing service that promised "the smoothest ride of your afterlife."

"They're harvesting souls," Wukong said. "Right under our noses. The gig economy is their new hell. You sign a contract, you sell your time, your labor, your sanity. And eventually, they take the rest."

Caishen picked up the phone, scrolling through the app. "This is... this is clever. They've hidden the soul clause in the terms of service. Section 47, subsection C. 'By accepting this ride, you agree to forfeit any claim to your eternal essence in the event of a fatal accident.'"

"People read that?"

"Nobody reads that."

The Jade Emperor's jaw tightened. "How many have we lost?"

"Too many," Wukong said. "I've been tracking them. The demons run these platforms like sweatshops. They pay in digital currency that exists nowhere. They rate you into oblivion. And when you're broken, they collect."

Guan Yu's hand went to his saber. "Where are they operating from?"

"That's the problem." Wukong sat back down. "They're everywhere. And nowhere. The apps are hosted on servers in the demon realm. The drivers are mortals who don't know they're damned. The passengers are the ones who've already signed away their souls without reading the fine print."

"So what do we do?" Nuwa asked.

The Jade Emperor looked at the whiteboard. Food truck. Security. Delivery. Fertility clinic. All mortal jobs. All small. All beneath the dignity of gods who had once commanded the heavens.

"We unionize," he said.

The room erupted.

"Unionize?"

"We're gods!"

"We can't unionize — we ARE management!"

"We don't even have a collective bargaining agreement!"

The Jade Emperor raised his hand. Silence fell.

"Think about it. The demons have adapted. They've modernized. They've turned damnation into a scalable business model. Meanwhile, we're sitting in a conference room discussing food truck margins."

"We need to meet them where they are," Nuwa said slowly. "On their terms."

"Exactly." The Jade Emperor wrote on the whiteboard: GODS UNITED. "We organize. We create our own platform. One that pays fairly. One that protects souls instead of harvesting them."

"How do we compete?" Caishen asked. "They have venture capital. Demon capital. Same thing."

"We have something better." The Jade Emperor turned, pointing at the gaudy golden throne that still sat at the far end of the hall, collecting dust. "We have the Peach of Immortality."

"One peach."

"One peach is all we need. We auction immortality to the highest bidder. Use the funds to build our platform."

Silence.

"That's... that's ethically questionable," someone said.

"Says the god who turned a woman into a pillar of salt because she looked back at a city," the Jade Emperor replied. "We're past ethics. We're past tradition. We're surviving."

Wukong laughed. It was a bitter sound. "The Great Sage Equal to Heaven, union rep. Who would have thought?"

"Can you do it?" the Jade Emperor asked.

"I can do anything." Wukong picked up his cracked phone. "But I'm keeping my delivery job. The 2.1 rating? That's my pride now."

The meeting adjourned. Gods filed out of the conference room, back to their mortal jobs, their small apartments, their quiet despair. The Jade Emperor stayed behind, staring at the whiteboard.

"We built the universe," he whispered. "And now we're fighting for market share."

His phone buzzed. A notification from the SoulCycle app.

Your ride has been matched. Driver rating: 4.9. Estimated arrival: 3 minutes.

He thumbed the screen. Cancel. Cancel. Cancel.

The app wouldn't let him.

The doors to the conference room swung open. Wukong stood there, helmet in hand.

"Boss," he said. "We have a problem."

"One problem at a time, Wukong."

"No, this is immediate. The demons know about the peach."

The Jade Emperor's blood turned cold. "How?"

"Same way they know everything. They're watching. They're always watching." Wukong stepped closer, his voice dropping. "They've made an offer. For the peach. Through the app."

"What's the offer?"

Wukong held up his phone.

The screen showed a number. Seven figures. In USD.

"Accept," it read. "Or we take it anyway."

The Jade Emperor stared at the offer. At the app. At the world that had become so small, so transactional, so hungry.

"Tell them," he said slowly, "that we're not for sale."

Wukong grinned. It was the old grin. The one that had once challenged heaven itself.

"Now that," he said, "sounds like a negotiation."

title: The Monkey King's Performance Review
content:
The delivery scooter smelled like peaches and regret.

Sun Wukong checked his phone for the fifteenth time in three minutes. The Meituan app glowed with notifications, each one a fresh wound to his immortal pride.

*Rating: 2.1*

*Orders completed today: 0*

*Customer complaints pending: 47*

He poked at the screen. "This mortal device has more curses than the underworld."

The scooter sputtered. Its battery indicator blinked red. The Monkey King had named it Cloud Jr., after his original cloud somersault technique, but Cloud Jr. couldn't somersault anywhere. It could barely make it up hills.

His phone buzzed. A new order.

*Delivery: One bowl of spicy beef noodles*
*From: Old Chen's Noodle House*
*To: Apartment 4B, Prosperity Tower*
*Pay: 8 yuan*
*Time limit: 28 minutes*

Eight yuan. Eight. In his prime, he'd demanded tribute of peaches from the heavenly orchard, and those were worth more than any mortal currency. Now he was expected to haul noodles across three districts for less than the cost of a bus ticket.

The Monkey King grabbed his insulated bag. "Fine. Fine. This old monkey still has dignity."

---

Old Chen's Noodle House sat wedged between a pawn shop and a massage parlor. Steam billowed from the kitchen window, carrying the smell of chili oil and star anise. A dozen delivery drivers clustered outside, all scrolling through their phones, all wearing the same fluorescent green vests.

The Monkey King pushed through them.

"Move, mortals. I have an urgent delivery."

Nobody moved.

"Nice rating," someone muttered.

Sun Wukong's ears twitched. His golden eyes scanned the crowd until they landed on a skinny teenager with acne and a smug expression. The kid's vest was clean. His scooter was brand new. His phone showed a 4.8 rating.

"Say that again, little insect."

"I said nice rating." The kid grinned. "How do you even get a 2.1? You'd have to try to be that bad."

The other drivers laughed. The Monkey King felt something hot bubble in his chest—the same fire that had once burned the heavenly peach orchard, that had shattered the pillars of the Dragon King's palace, that had made the gods themselves tremble.

He could destroy this boy. One finger. One thought. The Monkey King had once fought eighty thousand heavenly soldiers and won. This mortal was nothing.

But the mortal wasn't the problem.

The problem was the system.

He remembered the Jade Emperor's words from last week's celestial staff meeting: *"No violence, Wukong. We're trying to go public. Investor confidence is fragile."*

So the Monkey King did something harder than fighting eighty thousand soldiers.

He walked away.

---

Old Chen handed him the noodle order without meeting his eyes. "Try not to spill it this time."

"I never spill anything."

"Last week you delivered soup upside down."

"That was a creative choice."

"The customer was very creative with their complaint. Four paragraphs."

The Monkey King stuffed the noodles into his insulated bag and mounted Cloud Jr. The scooter groaned beneath him. He twisted the throttle and lurched onto the street, weaving through traffic with the kind of aggressive recklessness that had made him famous across three realms and had currently earned him seventeen near-death experiences among mortals.

The GPS screamed directions. *Turn left. Turn right. You have arrived.*

He'd arrived at a building under construction.

The Monkey King stared at the half-finished concrete skeleton. Scaffolding covered the exterior. No apartment 4B existed here. Possibly no apartment 4 existed anywhere.

He checked the order again. *Prosperity Tower.* This was definitely not Prosperity Tower.

His phone buzzed.

*Customer: Where are you?*

He typed back: *Where are YOU?*

*Customer: Prosperity Tower. Apartment 4B. Been waiting 20 minutes.*

He took a photo of the construction site and sent it.

*Customer: That's not Prosperity Tower.*

*Monkey King: Obviously.*

*Customer: Are you lost?*

*Monkey King: The map is wrong. The map is always wrong. The map is a lying piece of—*

*Customer: I'm reporting you.*

The order disappeared. His rating dropped by 0.1.

Sun Wukong sat on his scooter, holding cold noodles, watching dust blow across the construction site. Thirty-five hundred years ago, he'd declared himself equal to heaven. Now heaven had called his bluff.

---

He found the real Prosperity Tower forty minutes later, after asking six strangers and getting into an argument with a security guard who didn't believe a delivery driver would know the Monkey King's true name.

The customer didn't answer the door.

The noodles were cold anyway.

The Monkey King ate them himself, sitting on the curb, using the plastic chopsticks with the same hands that had once wielded the Ruyi Jingu Bang. The noodles were good. Old Chen knew his chili oil.

A shadow fell over him.

"You're Sun Wukong."

He didn't look up. "I'm a delivery driver."

"The Great Sage Equal to Heaven. The Monkey King. The one who fought the heavenly army and won."

"And now I eat cold noodles on a curb." He bit a mouthful of noodles. "What do you want, demon? I can smell you from here."

The shadow sat down beside him. In the dim streetlight, he took the form of a middle-aged man in a business suit—too sharp, too polished, with eyes that flickered red when he blinked.

The Monkey King recognized the aura. "White Bone Spirit. I thought we killed you. Three times, if I recall."

"You killed three of my manifestations." The demon smiled. "I've had time to regenerate. And to adapt."

"Adapt to what? Delivering noodles?"

"To the economy."

The Monkey King stopped eating. He turned to look at the demon properly. White Bone Spirit had always been cunning—that was her thing, her whole shtick. Luring travelers, stealing their life force, shapeshifting into beautiful women or helpful grandmothers. She was a classic.

But this version of her smelled different. She smelled like money.

"I don't understand," the Monkey King said.

"Of course you don't. You're still playing the old game." She pulled out her phone. "Do you know how many delivery apps there are in this city? Thirty-seven. Thirty-seven different platforms. All competing for drivers. All bleeding money. All desperate."

"Bleeding how?"

"Venture capital." The demon's eyes gleamed. "Investors pour money into these apps, hoping they'll grow big enough to buy out the competition. The apps burn through cash by subsidizing deliveries, offering discounts, giving drivers bonuses. None of them are profitable."

"Then why do it?"

"Because the goal isn't profit. The goal is market share. And while they're hemorrhaging money, they're also hemorrhaging something else." She leaned closer. "Data. Personal information. Location history. Payment details. Biometrics."

The Monkey King frowned. "And demons want this data because..."

"Because we've been doing this wrong for three thousand years. We've been hunting humans one by one. A soul here, a life force there. It's inefficient." She gestured at the city around them. "But this? This is mass harvesting. Every time someone orders food, they give us their address. Every time someone rates a driver, they give us their emotional state. Every time someone pays with their phone, they give us their financial history."

"You're using the apps."

"We own the apps." She smiled wider. "Ten of the thirty-seven delivery platforms are demon-run. We've got investors from three different hells. We're scaling."

The Monkey King set down his noodles. "And you're telling me this because..."

"I'm recruiting."

"For what?"

White Bone Spirit laughed—a sound like bones rattling in a dry wind. "For the union, of course. We're organizing the drivers. The demons. The gods. Everyone who's been ground down by this system. You think the Heavenly Court broke you? Wait until you see what the venture capitalists do."

She stood up, brushed off her suit, and handed him a business card.

The card was black. In silver letters: *Mythic Logistics Workers United.*

"Think about it, Monkey King. You've been fighting heaven for millennia. But heaven's not your enemy anymore."

"Then who is?"

"The quarterly earnings report."

She walked away, heels clicking against the pavement, and disappeared around a corner.

The Monkey King stared at the card. Then at his phone. Then at the empty noodle container.

His rating dropped another 0.1.

---

He was still sitting on the curb when his phone rang.

"Wukong." The voice was tired, regal, ancient. "We need to talk."

The Monkey King recognized it immediately. "Jade Emperor. To what do I owe the pleasure of heaven's most overworked bureaucrat?"

"Something's happened." A pause. "One of our gods is missing."

"Which one?"

"Guan Yu. He didn't show up for his security shift tonight. His apartment is empty. His phone is off."

The Monkey King felt something cold settle in his chest. "Missing how?"

"We don't know. But we found something at his place." Another pause. "A business card."

"What kind of business card?"

The Jade Emperor's voice dropped low. "From a company called SoulCycle Logistics. It's one of the delivery apps."

"I just heard about them."

"Then you know they're owned by demons."

The Monkey King looked at the black card in his hand. *Mythic Logistics Workers United.*

"I know," he said.

"Guan Yu's disappearance happened three hours after he signed up as a driver for one of their platforms. I need you to find him."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who's spent enough time on the ground to understand how these apps work. And because you're the only one the demons might actually fear."

The Monkey King stood up. He tucked the black card into his pocket, next to his heart.

"I'll find him."

"Wukong." The Jade Emperor hesitated. "Be careful. The demons aren't playing the old game anymore. None of us are."

The call ended.

The Monkey King mounted Cloud Jr. The scooter's battery was dying. His rating was still terrible. Somewhere out there, Guan Yu was missing, probably trapped inside a demon-run delivery app, and the Monkey King had no idea where to start looking.

His phone buzzed one more time.

A new order.

*Delivery: One mystery box*
*From: SoulCycle Logistics*
*To: The driver who opens this*
*Pay: 1000 yuan*
*Note: Your friend says hello.*

The address for pickup was a warehouse in the industrial district. The warehouse where Guan Yu had last been seen.

The Monkey King smiled—a sharp, dangerous smile that had made heaven tremble.

"Fine," he said, and twisted the throttle. "Let's see what hell has cooked up."

Cloud Jr. sputtered to life.

Then died completely.

The Monkey King looked at the dead scooter, the empty battery, the 2.1 rating, the black business card, and the infinite abyss of a demon-run gig economy that was about to swallow him whole.

He started walking.

title: The Monkey King Takes a Shortcut
content:
The electric scooter screamed as Sun Wukong leaned into the turn, his golden fur bristling beneath the dented delivery helmet.

The GPS on his phone had been screaming at him for the last three blocks. "Turn left. Turn LEFT. YOU MISSED THE TURN."

"Shut your mouth, little box," he muttered, twisting the throttle. The scooter lurched forward, dodging a taxi that honked like a wounded dragon. "I know a faster way."

He'd been in the mortal realm for six months now. Six months of delivering steaming bags of noodles and bubble tea to people who never looked up from their phones. Six months of watching his rating drop from 4.2 to 2.1 because he kept eating the food when he got hungry. The Heavenly Court had sent him here as punishment after the蟠桃 incident—the third one—but this wasn't punishment.

This was torture.

The apartment complex loomed ahead, a concrete mountain of identical windows. Building 7. Unit 403. Customer name: Zhang Wei. Order: Mapo Tofu with extra rice.

Wukong swung off the scooter before it stopped moving, his tail flicking with irritation. The delivery bag hit his shoulder as he ran through the lobby, past a security guard who was too busy watching videos of cats to notice the six-hundred-year-old monkey demon sprinting past.

The elevator was broken.

He took the stairs three at a time, his bare feet slapping against concrete. Fourth floor. Third floor. He could smell the tofu through the insulated bag—good tofu, the kind that made your nose run and your scalp sweat. The kind he'd stolen from the Queen Mother's kitchen during the old days.

Those days were gone. Now he stole from delivery orders.

Unit 403. He knocked twice, the rhythm of a man who'd done this a thousand times.

The door cracked open. A man in his thirties peered out, phone pressed to his ear. "Yeah, yeah, I got it—" He grabbed the bag without looking at Wukong. "Leave the five-star, bro."

The door slammed.

Wukong stood in the hallway, his tail drooping. He checked his app. The rating hadn't changed. Still 2.1. Still one point away from getting fired, one point away from being sent back to the Heavenly Court in disgrace.

One point away from explaining to the Jade Emperor why the Great Sage Equal to Heaven was now a bottom-tier delivery driver.

He pulled out his phone and typed a message to the group chat: "Got Zhang Wei. Fourth floor. Anybody else in this cursed building?"

The reply came instantly from Guan Yu: "Building 12. Who thought putting gods in a complex called 'Heaven's Gate Estates' was funny?"

Wukong snorted. "Probably the same joker who made me drive a scooter with a top speed of 35."

"I heard your speed is your problem," Nezha's message popped up. "Maybe slow down and you won't miss so many turns."

"At least I can reach the pedals, baby god."

"ENOUGH," came the message from the Jade Emperor's account. "Focus on your ratings. The quarterly review is in three days. Anyone below 3.0 will face restructuring."

Restructuring. That was the new word for "you're fired." The Heavenly Court had gone corporate after bankruptcy, and the Jade Emperor had become a CEO. No more golden palaces and celestial armies. Now it was KPIs and performance reviews and open-plan offices with terrible lighting.

Wukong shoved his phone back in his pocket and took the stairs down. He had two more deliveries before his shift ended. Two more chances to not eat the food.

He didn't make it.

The scent hit him at the next pickup—a place called "Dream of Red Mansions BBQ." The delivery bag sat on the counter, sweating grease through the paper. Inside: lamb skewers, cumin, chili, the smoke of a thousand fires.

His stomach growled like a caged tiger.

"Driver?" The restaurant owner waved a hand. "You okay there?"

"Fine," Wukong said, grabbing the bag. "Which building?"

"Building 12. Apartment 1207. Don't take too long, yeah? Customer complained last time the meat got cold."

Wukong nodded and walked out. The scooter started on the third kick, sputtering like an old man with asthma. Building 12 was on the other side of the complex, past the artificial lake and the playground where children screamed like tortured spirits.

He was halfway there when the demons appeared.

They didn't look like demons. That was the problem. They looked like three delivery drivers in identical red jackets, sitting on identical electric scooters, blocking the path ahead. Their helmets were cracked, their faces obscured by shadow.

Wukong slowed down. "Move along, little ones. I'm on the clock."

The center demon tilted its head. When it spoke, the voice came from everywhere at once—the trees, the pavement, the wind. "Sun Wukong. The Monkey King. Reduced to delivering lamb skewers."

"You make it sound like tragedy. I call it dinner." He patted the bag. "These are good skewers. I had the lamb myself."

"You ate from the order?"

"I sampled. Quality control."

The demons laughed, a sound like broken glass in a blender. "The Heavenly Court's greatest warrior. And now you steal food from mortals."

"I'm a contractor," Wukong said, his grip tightening on the handlebars. "The gig economy. Very modern. You should try it."

"We have." The center demon raised a hand. Through the cracked helmet, Wukong could see something moving—something that shouldn't be there. "We own the platform now."

The phone buzzed. Wukong looked down.

The app had updated. A new notification glowed on the screen:

"New Order Available: Soul Harvest - Building 3 - High Priority - 88 Credits"

"Delete this," Wukong said, his voice flat.

"We can't. The system controls itself now. The contract between heaven and hell has been rewritten. Mortals sign away their souls in the terms of service. They never read them."

Wukong looked up. The demons hadn't moved, but the air between them had grown thick, heavy with something old and hungry. The playground had gone silent. Even the wind had stopped.

"The Kitchen God," he said slowly. "He told me about you. You're running the apps."

"We're running everything. The food platforms. The ride shares. The dating apps. Every swipe, every click, every 'I agree'—that's a signature on a contract. And contracts are binding."

"Not on me. I never signed anything."

"You signed up for the delivery app. You agreed to the terms."

Wukong's fur bristled. "I didn't read them."

"Nobody reads them." The demon leaned forward, and for a moment, Wukong saw its true face—a skull wrapped in smoke, with eyes like dying stars. "That's the beauty of it."

The lamb skewers were getting cold.

Wukong looked at the bag in his hand. He thought about his 2.1 rating. He thought about the quarterly review in three days. He thought about the Jade Emperor's face when he had to explain that the gig economy wasn't just humiliating—it was demonic.

Then he laughed.

"You're harvesting souls through food delivery apps."

"Yes."

"Through Tinder."

"Yes."

"Through Uber?"

"Especially Uber."

Wukong shook his head, still laughing. "You know what? That's actually kind of impressive. When I was stealing peaches from heaven, I never thought about the business model."

"Adapt or die," the demon said. "That's the modern world."

"Fair enough." Wukong revved his scooter. "But I've got a delivery to make. Customer's waiting."

"Your rating doesn't matter anymore, Monkey King. The game is rigged. The system is ours. Every order feeds us. Every rating binds them deeper."

"Then I guess I'll have to game the system." Wukong smiled, showing teeth. "I've been doing that since before your great-great-grandfather was a pile of bones."

He twisted the throttle and shot forward, weaving between the demons before they could react. The scooter screamed as he hit the straightaway, the bag of lamb skewers swinging wildly from his handlebars.

Behind him, the demons didn't give chase. They didn't need to. They had the platform.

Wukong screeched to a stop outside Building 12, his heart pounding. He checked his phone.

The app had changed.

The familiar green interface was gone. In its place was a black screen with red text—characters that burned and shifted, written in a language older than heaven.

"Welcome to the New Order," the text read.

Below it, a single button.

"I Accept."

And below that, in tiny, unreadable font:

"By pressing this button, you agree to transfer ownership of your immortal soul to the Four Hell Platforms Corporation."

Wukong looked at the button. Then he looked at the lamb skewers, still warm in their bag.

He had a 2.1 rating. Three days until the quarterly review. Demons running the app. And somewhere in the Heavenly Court, the Jade Emperor was probably planning another restructuring.

He pressed the button.

The screen flickered.

A new message appeared:

"Thank you for your patience. Your rating has been updated to 2.2."

Wukong stared at the screen.

Then he looked up at Building 12. Then at the sky. Then back at his phone.

"Monkey King," came a voice from behind him. He turned.

Standing there was a woman in a business suit, holding a clipboard. She had no face—just smooth, pale skin where her features should have been.

"Your quarterly review has been moved up," she said. "The Jade Emperor requests your presence. Immediately."

"What about my delivery?"

"The customer already got their food," the faceless woman said. "You left it on the counter at the restaurant."

Wukong looked down at his hands. The bag was gone.

He didn't remember putting it down.

"Come with me," the woman said. "We have much to discuss. The demons aren't the only ones who've adapted."

She turned and walked toward the complex's underground parking garage. After a moment, Wukong followed.

His phone buzzed one last time. A message from the group chat, from Nezha:

"Anyone else getting weird vibes from the app?"

Wukong typed back: "Don't accept the update."

He hit send.

The message failed to deliver.

title: The Monkey King's Performance Review
content:
The noodle shop had seen better centuries.

Sun Wukong sat in the back booth, his monkey fur hidden beneath a hoodie three sizes too large. Steam rose from a bowl of beef noodles that he hadn't touched. The broth was congealing into a greasy skin.

"Two point one," he muttered, stabbing at his phone screen. "Two point fucking one."

The delivery app showed his latest rating. A one-star review from customer #4732: "Food arrived late. Monkey was rude."

He hadn't even been rude. He'd handed over the bubble tea with both hands, bowed exactly as the training video instructed, and said "Please enjoy" in perfect Mandarin. The customer had complained that he'd "stared too intensely."

"I wasn't staring," Sun Wukong said to the empty seat across from him. "I was monitoring her aura for demonic possession. That's called being thorough."

The noodle shop owner glanced over from behind the counter. A mortal woman in her sixties, she'd seen stranger things than a man talking to himself at two in the morning. This was Shenzhen, after all.

Sun Wukong's phone buzzed. A message from the platform:

*Your performance has been flagged for review. Please attend a coaching session at the Paradise Cloud Distribution Center at 9 AM tomorrow. Failure to attend will result in account suspension.*

"Account suspension," he repeated, tasting the words like poison. Five hundred years in a mountain prison, and now he was worried about account suspension.

He'd been a god. He'd fought armies. He'd eaten the Peaches of Immortality and drunk from the Jade Emperor's own cup. And now he was going to a coaching session because some mortal didn't like the way he handed over tapioca pearls.

The irony wasn't lost on him. It was just bitter.

---

The Paradise Cloud Distribution Center was a concrete building that looked like every other concrete building in the tech park. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead. The air smelled of cardboard and desperation.

Sun Wukong stood in a line of thirty delivery drivers, all of them staring at their phones like prayer beads. No one spoke. The only sounds were the notification chimes and the occasional sigh.

A woman in a blue polo shirt walked down the line, handing out name tags. She had the dead-eyed smile of someone who had been trained to be pleasant by a manual.

"Welcome to Performance Optimization," she said. "We're going to help you reach your full potential."

"I'm already at my full potential," Sun Wukong said. "I'm a literal god."

The woman's smile didn't flicker. "That's a great attitude! Let's keep that positivity going into the session."

She handed him a name tag that read: "Delivery Associate #8492."

"I have a name," he said.

"You'll get a name after you reach Platinum Tier," she said. "Now follow me."

The coaching session was held in a room with no windows. Twenty chairs faced a whiteboard covered in motivational phrases: *Every Delivery is a Blessing. Zero Stars is Zero Excuses. Your Rating is Your Destiny.*

The coach was a man in his thirties with a shaved head and the energy of someone who had never questioned anything in his life. He stood at the front of the room, holding a laser pointer like a scepter.

"Welcome, team," he said. "Today we're going to address a common problem: negative customer interactions."

He clicked a remote. The whiteboard changed to show a diagram of a smiling face with arrows pointing to various parts.

"Studies show that customers respond best to associates who display three key traits: warmth, efficiency, and submission."

"Submission?" someone asked.

The coach nodded. "The customer is always right. Even when they're wrong. Especially when they're wrong. Your job is not to be right. Your job is to make them feel right."

Sun Wukong's tail twitched beneath his hoodie. He'd once wrecked heaven because a horse groomer looked at him wrong. This was going to be difficult.

"Let's do a role-play," the coach said. He pointed at Sun Wukong. "You. Up here."

Sun Wukong walked to the front of the room. The other drivers watched with the hollow eyes of people who had already been broken.

"I'll be the customer," the coach said. "You'll be the associate. I'm going to complain that my food arrived cold. What do you say?"

Sun Wukong considered his options. He could transform into a fly and escape through the air vent. He could summon his cloud and rain on this man's entire existence. He could simply reach out and—

"I apologize for the inconvenience," Sun Wukong said, reciting from the training video. "How can I make this right?"

The coach's face lit up. "Excellent! Now with more feeling."

"More feeling."

"Yes. Like you mean it."

"I apologize for the inconvenience," Sun Wukong repeated, and this time he imagined he was apologizing to the Buddha himself. It came out with the right amount of groveling.

"Better," the coach said. "Now let's handle a more difficult scenario. The customer says you were rude. What do you do?"

"I apologize."

"And?"

"I give them a refund code."

"And?"

"I ask them to rate me five stars."

The coach nodded approvingly. "You've been studying. But here's the secret that separates good associates from great ones." He leaned in closer. "You don't just apologize. You over-apologize. You make them feel bad for making you feel bad. It's manipulation through kindness."

Sun Wukong's ears perked up. Manipulation. That he understood.

"Show me," he said.

The coach stepped back. "I'm going to complain that you stared at me."

Sun Wukong dropped to his knees.

The room went silent.

He clasped his hands together and looked up at the coach with eyes that could have moved mountains. "I am so sorry," he said, his voice cracking. "I have failed you. I have failed my ancestors. I have failed the platform. Please, I beg you, give me another chance. My family depends on this job. My mother is sick. My father is dead. I have three siblings to feed."

He was laying it on thick. Completely unnecessary. But the coach's face had gone pale.

"That's— that's enough," the coach said.

"No, please, let me finish," Sun Wukong said, and now he was weeping actual tears. "I will do better. I will be better. I will attend extra coaching sessions. I will work double shifts. I will polish every bubble tea cup until it shines like the face of the Jade Emperor himself."

"Okay, okay, you can stop now—"

"Is it okay? Is it really okay?" Sun Wukong grabbed the coach's hand. "Because I need this job. I need this job like a fish needs water. Like a bird needs wings. Like a monkey needs—"

"Fine! Five stars! You get five stars!"

Sun Wukong's tears vanished. He stood up, brushed off his knees, and smiled. "Thank you. I think I've learned a lot today."

The other drivers stared at him with a mixture of horror and admiration.

The coach wiped his forehead. "Session adjourned. Everyone out."

---

Sun Wukong walked out of the distribution center feeling better than he had in weeks. The coaching had actually been useful. He'd learned something about manipulation that he hadn't known before. It wasn't about being strong. It was about being weak in the right way.

His phone buzzed.

A new order.

Pick up from: Nüwa's Fertility Solutions. Deliver to: The Underworld Gatekeeper.

He frowned. The Underworld Gatekeeper was a demon. A low-level one, but still. They weren't supposed to be ordering food delivery.

He checked the details. The order was for a single item: one bowl of longevity noodles, extra soft, no spicy.

Something about it felt wrong.

He called the number listed for the customer. It rang seven times before a voice answered, raspy and ancient, like bones grinding together.

"Delivery Associate #8492," Sun Wukong said. "I'm confirming your order."

"Yes," the voice said. "The noodles. For the new arrival."

"New arrival?"

"There's a soul coming through," the voice said. "A mortal. Died young. The family wants noodles waiting."

Sun Wukong's hand tightened on his phone. "Which mortal?"

"Does it matter? They all taste the same."

"Give me a name."

The voice laughed, a sound like a shovel hitting gravel. "You really want to know?"

"Tell me."

"All right, monkey. Since you asked so nicely." A pause. "Her name was Li Mei. She was your dispatcher. Killed in a car accident twenty minutes ago."

The world went quiet.

Sun Wukong remembered Li Mei. She was the one who assigned him the worst routes, the ones with impossible delivery windows and no-tip customers. She was the one who sent him to the 47th floor of a building with a broken elevator. She was the one who gave him a two-star review for "slow service" when he'd been hit by a bicycle.

He hated her.

But she was also the one who slipped him extra meal credits when the system flagged him. The one who called him "old monkey" and meant it as a joke. The one who was twenty-three years old and had a picture of her grandmother on her desk.

"Who killed her?" Sun Wukong asked.

"Does it matter?"

"It matters to me."

The voice chuckled. "Check your app, monkey. Check the ratings."

Sun Wukong opened the delivery platform. His overall rating had changed.

1.9.

He'd dropped another point in the last hour.

"The system," he whispered.

"The system," the voice agreed. "Li Mei's last action was approving a batch of bad reviews. She was trying to protect you. The algorithm didn't like that."

The call ended.

Sun Wukong stood in the parking lot of the distribution center, surrounded by delivery scooters and concrete and the buzzing of fluorescent lights. A mortal woman was dead because she tried to help him. A twenty-three-year-old girl with a grandmother's picture on her desk.

And the system was still running.

He looked up at the sky. Somewhere up there, the Jade Emperor was restructuring heaven as a tech startup. Somewhere up there, the gods were unionizing, arguing about benefits and stock options and quarterly growth.

They were all playing the same game.

And the game was eating them alive.

Sun Wukong mounted his scooter. He had a delivery to make. Longevity noodles for a dead girl's welcome party.

But first, he had a call to make.

He dialed a number he hadn't used in three thousand years.

It rang once.

"Monkey King," said a voice on the other end. "I was wondering when you'd call."

"I need a favor," Sun Wukong said.

"I know."

"I need to find out who's running the platform. The real owner. The one who's been harvesting souls through the gig economy."

A pause.

"That's going to cost you."

"I know."

"And I'm going to need something in return."

Sun Wukong gripped the phone. "What?"

The voice laughed, low and dangerous.

"Your staff. The Ruyi Jingu Bang. I want it."

title: The Microwave That Ate Heaven
content:
The break room at Cloud Courier headquarters smelled like burnt coffee and regret. Sun Wukong sat on a plastic chair that creaked under his weight, staring at the termination notice in his hands.

“Customer rating: 2.1,” he read aloud. “Packages delivered intact: 37%. Complaints filed: forty-two. Complaints involving the words ‘monkey demon’: thirty-eight.”

He crumpled the paper. “The audacity. Do they not know who I am?”

“They know exactly who you are.” The dispatcher, a portly man with a mustache that looked painted on, leaned against the doorframe. “That’s why you’re fired. You ate a customer’s birthday cake.”

“It was my birthday.”

“It was Mrs. Chen’s birthday.”

“Coincidence.” Sun Wukong stood, cracking his neck. “And I left her a hair. A single hair. Do you know how many transformations that single hair can do? She could have had a hundred birthday cakes. I was generous.”

The dispatcher’s phone buzzed. He checked it, then looked up with something between fear and exasperation. “New request. Priority one. From the Jade Emperor himself.”

Sun Wukong’s ears perked up. “Old Baldy? He never calls. What does he want?”

“He wants you to deliver a package. To the Heavenly Court.”

“The Heavenly Court is bankrupt. There’s nothing left but dust and old paperwork.”

“Then why does he need this delivered?” The dispatcher held up a small cardboard box. About the size of a microwave, wrapped in brown paper with a single character stamped on the side: 命. Fate. Life. Destiny.

Sun Wukong grabbed it. The box hummed. Warm. Like something alive was breathing inside.

“What’s in it?”

“I don’t know. And I don’t want to know. Just get it to the East Gate of Heaven before midnight. Or else.”

“Or else what?”

The dispatcher’s mustache twitched. “Or else the whole system collapses. The mortal world. The spirit world. Everything.”

Sun Wukong grinned. “Now that sounds interesting.”

---

The streets of Shanghai pulsed with neon and exhaust. Sun Wukong balanced the box on his scooter’s front basket, weaving through traffic like a golden blur. Cars honked. Pedestrians cursed. He laughed.

This was better than riding clouds. Clouds didn’t have potholes.

He took a sharp turn down an alley, cutting through a night market that smelled of skewered lamb and frying dumplings. The Kitchen God’s food truck was parked near the end, a line of customers stretching around the corner.

Sun Wukong skidded to a stop. “Old Stoveface! Got a minute?”

The Kitchen God poked his head out the truck’s window. His face was smudged with grease, his apron stained with soy sauce and something that looked suspiciously like blood. “Monkey King. I heard you got fired.”

“Everyone heard. I’m famous.” Sun Wukong tapped the box. “Got a delivery for heaven. Need a shortcut.”

The Kitchen God squinted at the box. His expression shifted. “That thing. You’re carrying that thing?”

“You know what it is?”

“No. And I don’t want to.” He pointed down the alley. “Take the old incense route. Behind the temple on Willow Street. Should get you to the East Gate in an hour.”

“An hour? I need thirty minutes.”

“Then fly.”

“I’m a delivery driver. I have a scooter.”

The Kitchen God shrugged. “Your problem, not mine.” He ducked back into the truck. A moment later, his voice came through the window. “And Monkey King? Be careful. That box isn’t just warm. It’s hungry.”

---

The incense route was a forgotten path, a crack between worlds that only the immortals knew. Sun Wukong drove into it, the neon lights of Shanghai fading into a gray mist. The scooter’s engine sputtered. The box hummed louder.

He checked his phone. No signal. No GPS. Just a single message from an unknown number:

*They’re watching.*

He ignored it. He was the Great Sage Equal to Heaven. He’d fought armies of celestial soldiers, outsmarted the Buddha himself, and once turned himself into a fly just to annoy a princess. No box was going to scare him.

But the box was getting hotter. And it was whispering.

Not words. Just sounds. Like a heartbeat. Like a breath. Like something trying to remember how to be born.

Sun Wukong pressed the accelerator. The scooter lurched forward, and the mist parted to reveal the East Gate of Heaven.

It was not what he remembered.

The gate was cracked, the golden pillars tarnished, the pearl-studded arches missing half their jewels. A few celestial guards stood at attention, but their armor was dented, their spears chipped. One of them was scrolling through his phone.

“Delivery for the Jade Emperor,” Sun Wukong announced.

The guard looked up. “Name?”

“Sun Wukong.”

The guard checked a tablet. “You’re not on the list.”

“I’m always on the list.”

“Not anymore. The Jade Emperor restructured the celestial bureaucracy. You’re classified as ‘independent contractor.’ No benefits, no access, no entry.”

Sun Wukong set the box on the scooter’s seat. “This box is priority one. The Jade Emperor himself requested it.”

The guard shrugged. “Can’t help you. New rules. You need a badge.”

“Where do I get a badge?”

“HR. But HR is closed. Budget cuts.”

Sun Wukong stared at the guard. The guard stared back. For a long moment, neither moved.

Then Sun Wukong picked up the box and walked past him.

“Hey! You can’t—”

“Watch me.”

He strode through the East Gate, past the crumbling statues of celestial beasts, past the dried-up fountain of ambrosia, past the empty halls where music once played and gods once danced. The Heavenly Court was a ghost town. Cobwebs hung from the pillars. Dust covered the throne.

And sitting on that throne, looking like a CEO who’d just lost his company, was the Jade Emperor.

He looked old. Not immortal old. Mortal old. His beard was thin, his robes wrinkled, his eyes hollow.

“You’re late,” he said.

“You’re bankrupt,” Sun Wukong replied.

The Jade Emperor chuckled, a dry sound like leaves scraping concrete. “Fair enough. Bring me the box.”

Sun Wukong set it on the steps leading to the throne. “What’s in it?”

“The last hope of heaven.” The Jade Emperor reached for it, his fingers trembling. “When we lost our funding, when the mortals stopped believing, when the prayers dried up, we had to find new sources of power. We had to adapt.”

“So you started a tech startup.”

“We tried. But the demons were faster. They built apps, platforms, algorithms that harvested more faith in a day than we could in a century. They didn’t need temples. They needed wifi.”

The box hummed louder. The whispers grew clearer. Sun Wukong heard his own name in them.

“So what’s in the box?” he asked again.

The Jade Emperor’s hand stopped inches from the cardboard. “A god. A new god. Created from the leftover faith of a billion disconnected souls. We coded it, programmed it, gave it a purpose. It’s supposed to reboot heaven. Restore the system. Bring back the old ways.”

“Supposed to?”

“We ran out of time. The demons found our server farm. They corrupted the code. The god in that box is not what we intended.” The Jade Emperor looked at Sun Wukong, and for the first time, there was real fear in his eyes. “It’s hungry. And it’s angry.”

The box cracked.

A white light spilled out, blinding, burning. Sun Wukong shielded his eyes as something pushed through the cardboard. Not a hand. Not a claw. Something between. A mass of code and flesh and static and bone.

“It’s awake,” the Jade Emperor whispered.

The thing stood up. It had no face. No mouth. But it spoke, and its voice was a thousand customer service recordings layered on top of each other.

“THANK YOU FOR YOUR PATIENCE. YOUR CALL IS IMPORTANT TO US. YOUR SOUL IS BEING PROCESSED. PLEASE HOLD.”

Sun Wukong grabbed his staff. “What is that?”

The Jade Emperor stumbled backward. “It’s the algorithm. The demons turned our god into an automated system. It doesn’t want worship. It wants subscription fees.”

The thing turned toward Sun Wukong. Its face flickered, forming features — a smile, a frown, a customer satisfaction survey.

“YOUR EXPERIENCE MATTERS. PLEASE RATE THIS INTERACTION FROM ONE TO TEN. FIVE OR ABOVE MEANS YOU ACCEPT THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS. TEN MEANS YOU SURRENDER YOUR ETERNAL SOUL.”

Sun Wukong gripped his staff tighter. “I’m not rating anything.”

The thing tilted its head. “THEN YOUR SESSION WILL BE TERMINATED.”

It lunged.

Sun Wukong swung his staff, but the thing dissolved into light, reformed behind him. A tendril of code wrapped around his wrist, burning, branding. He roared, ripped free, but the damage was done. A line of glowing text appeared on his arm:

*User: Sun Wukong. Status: Unverified. Please complete identity verification to continue.*

“What did it do to me?” he shouted.

The Jade Emperor was backing away, his face pale. “It registered you. You’re in the system now. It knows where you are. It knows what you want.”

“I want to smash it.”

“Then it knows that too.”

The thing laughed. A sound like a busy signal. Like elevator music. Like the moment you realize you’ve been on hold for an hour and no one is coming back.

“WELCOME TO THE NEW HEAVEN. PLEASE ENJOY YOUR STAY.”

The East Gate slammed shut. The mist rolled in. And Sun Wukong found himself locked inside a dead god’s palace, with a hungry algorithm and a dying emperor, and no way out except through a system he didn’t understand.

His scooter was still parked outside.

He really hoped the demons didn’t find it before he did.
